chapter_context.summary: Naaman, the Syrian army commander, is cured of leprosy by bathing seven times in the Jordan on Elisha's command; Gehazi is smitten with Naaman's leprosy for his deceitful greed.

outline.text: Naaman the Syrian Leper Sent to Elisha with Letters
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Naaman the Syrian Leper Sent to Elisha with Letters (5:1-7), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Kings.

outline.text: Naaman commanded to Wash Seven Times in Jordan; Cleansed
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Naaman commanded to Wash Seven Times in Jordan; Cleansed (5:8-14), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Kings.

outline.text: Naaman's Thanksgiving and Gift Refused by Elisha
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Naaman's Thanksgiving and Gift Refused by Elisha (5:15-19), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Kings.

outline.text: Gehazi's Greed; Secretly Obtains Gifts; Smitten with Leprosy
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Gehazi's Greed; Secretly Obtains Gifts; Smitten with Leprosy (5:20-27), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of 2 Kings.

key_themes.title: Prophetic Authority
key_themes.description: Elisha carries the mantle of Elijah, showing that God's word stands firm in judgment and mercy regardless of political power.

key_themes.title: Covenant Faithfulness and Exile
key_themes.description: The tragic fall of Samaria and Jerusalem shows that persistent rebellion and idolatry inevitably lead to judgment.

devotional.question: How do the events of Chapter 5 prompt you to trust in God's promises even during seasons of spiritual decline?
What warnings or instructions does this chapter hold for guarding your own heart against syncretism?

cultural_historical.title: The Assyrian and Babylonian Empires
cultural_historical.text: The rise of Mesopotamian super-empires (Assyria and Babylon) serves as the historical backdrop for the exile and division of the Hebrew kingdoms.
